questions.stem: A researcher stains a dividing human somatic cell and captures it at prophase of mitosis, after DNA synthesis is complete. How many chromatids are present in this cell?

explanation.summary: After S phase each of the 46 chromosomes carries two sister chromatids, so a prophase mitotic cell has 92 chromatids.
explanation.whyCorrect: During S phase DNA synthesis produces an identical copy of each DNA molecule, so each chromosome becomes two identical sister chromatids. At prophase of mitosis the cell still has 46 chromosomes but now contains 92 chromatids, double the amount of genetic material, ready for segregation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 23 chromatids would be found in a haploid gamete, not a somatic cell in mitosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 46 chromatids describes a cell in G1, before S phase replicates the DNA.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 69 does not correspond to any normal cell state.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 184 is twice the correct value and is not found in a normal cell.

questions.stem: A germ cell has completed meiosis I and is now in prophase II. How many chromatids does this cell contain?

explanation.summary: After meiosis I the homologs have separated to give 23 chromosomes, but each still has two sister chromatids, for 46 chromatids.
explanation.whyCorrect: In meiosis I homologous chromosomes segregate, so the daughter cell is haploid with 23 chromosomes. However, the sister chromatids do not separate until meiosis II, so each of the 23 chromosomes still consists of two identical chromatids, giving 46 total chromatids in prophase II.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 23 chromatids is the count only after meiosis II is complete, when sister chromatids have separated.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 69 does not correspond to any normal cell state.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 92 chromatids is the count in a diploid cell at prophase of mitosis, before meiosis I reduces the number.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 184 is not found in a normal cell.

questions.stem: You are asked to relay a cytogenetics report for a healthy 34-year-old man being evaluated for infertility. His peripheral blood karyotype reads 46,XY,t(9;22)(p22;q13). Which of the following is the most accurate interpretation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: He has a balanced translocation involving 9p and 22q

choices.label: B
choices.text: He has an unbalanced translocation involving 9p and 22q

choices.label: C
choices.text: He has a balanced translocation involving 9q and 22p

choices.label: D
choices.text: He has a Robertsonian translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22

choices.label: E
choices.text: He has an interstitial deletion of chromosome 22

explanation.summary: The 't' with a total of 46 chromosomes denotes a balanced reciprocal translocation, and the bands map in order to 9p and 22q.
explanation.whyCorrect: The abbreviation 't' indicates a translocation, and a total count of 46 chromosomes with no plus or minus sign indicates it is balanced, with no net gain or loss of material. The band coordinates in the second parentheses correspond in order to the chromosomes listed, so p22 belongs to chromosome 9 and q13 belongs to chromosome 22.

whyWrongByChoice.B: An unbalanced translocation implies unequal exchange with extra or missing material; the balanced 46-chromosome notation here excludes that.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The band order matches the chromosome order, so the segments are 9p and 22q, not 9q and 22p.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A Robertsonian translocation is denoted 'rob', involves only acrocentric chromosomes, and yields 45 chromosomes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A deletion would be denoted 'del', and this karyotype shows an exchange between two chromosomes, not a loss.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with chronic myeloid leukemia is found to have a balanced translocation in which no chromosomal material is gained or lost. Her physician explains that despite the balanced exchange, the rearrangement still causes disease. Which mechanism best accounts for this?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The exchange produces a monosomy of the involved chromosomes

choices.label: B
choices.text: The translocation deletes an entire acrocentric chromosome

choices.label: C
choices.text: A breakpoint joins two genes to create an abnormal fusion gene such as BCR-ABL

choices.label: D
choices.text: The rearrangement converts all euchromatin to heterochromatin across the genome

choices.label: E
choices.text: The translocation adds a third copy of every gene on both chromosomes

explanation.summary: Even a balanced translocation can be pathogenic when a breakpoint disrupts a gene or fuses two genes into an abnormal product.
explanation.whyCorrect: A balanced translocation has no net gain or loss of material, but the translocation breakpoints can still disrupt gene function. If the breakpoint falls within or between genes it can create a fusion gene, such as BCR-ABL in chronic myeloid leukemia, leading to abnormal function and disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A balanced translocation does not remove a whole chromosome, so no monosomy results.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Deletion of an entire chromosome is not a feature of a balanced reciprocal translocation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Position effects can locally alter expression, but a translocation does not convert the entire genome to heterochromatin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A balanced exchange does not triplicate genes; that would be a gain of material, making it unbalanced.

questions.stem: A chromosomal rearrangement occurs in which the short arms of two acrocentric chromosomes are lost and their long arms fuse to form a single large chromosome with two long arms. This describes which of the following?

explanation.summary: Fusion of the long arms of two acrocentric chromosomes with loss of the short arms defines a Robertsonian translocation.
explanation.whyCorrect: A Robertsonian translocation specifically refers to the fusion of the q (long) arms of two acrocentric chromosomes, with subsequent loss of the p (short) arms. Because the lost short arms carry only satellite DNA and redundant ribosomal RNA genes, the rearrangement is still considered balanced.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A deletion is simply the loss of a chromosome segment, not a fusion of two chromosomes.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A duplication is an extra copy of a portion of a chromosome, causing partial trisomy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A reciprocal translocation is an exchange of segments between two chromosomes, not a whole-arm fusion with arm loss.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An inversion flips a segment end to end within a single chromosome and does not fuse two chromosomes.

questions.stem: A couple with recurrent pregnancy loss undergoes karyotyping, and the woman is found to be a balanced carrier of a Robertsonian translocation reported as 45,XX,rob(13;14)(q10;q10). What is her total chromosome number?

explanation.summary: Balanced Robertsonian carriers have only 45 chromosomes because two acrocentric chromosomes fuse into one.
explanation.whyCorrect: In a Robertsonian translocation the long arms of two acrocentric chromosomes fuse into a single derivative chromosome and the short arms are lost. This reduces the total count by one, so balanced carriers have 45 chromosomes, exactly as the leading number in the karyotype indicates.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 23 is the haploid number found in a gamete, not a somatic carrier cell.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 44 would imply loss of two whole chromosomes; only one derivative chromosome is lost in the count.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 46 is the normal diploid number; the fusion reduces this by one.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 47 indicates an extra chromosome (aneuploidy), which is the opposite of this fusion.

questions.stem: A cytogeneticist is teaching which chromosomes can participate in a Robertsonian translocation, noting that only acrocentric chromosomes are involved. Which group correctly lists the human acrocentric autosomes?

explanation.summary: The acrocentric autosomes are 13, 14, 15, 21, and 22, and only these participate in Robertsonian translocations.
explanation.whyCorrect: Acrocentric chromosomes have a centromere almost at the end, leaving a tiny p arm; in humans these are chromosomes 13, 14, 15, 21, and 22. Their short arms contain satellite DNA and redundant ribosomal RNA genes, which is why their fusion in a Robertsonian translocation is still balanced.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 1, 3, 16, 19, and 20 are metacentric chromosomes with roughly equal arms.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 2, 4, 5, 17, and 18 are submetacentric chromosomes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 6 through 10 are submetacentric, not acrocentric.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 11, 12, and X are submetacentric; this group is not the acrocentric set.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old pregnant woman has an amniocentesis after ultrasound suggests symmetrical fetal growth restriction. The report returns as 46,XX,del(4)(p15.1p15.32). Which of the following best describes this chromosomal finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Terminal deletion of the long arm of chromosome 4

choices.label: B
choices.text: Interstitial deletion of the short arm of chromosome 4

choices.label: C
choices.text: Duplication of the short arm of chromosome 4

explanation.summary: The notation del(4)(p15.1p15.32) denotes an interstitial deletion of the short (p) arm of chromosome 4.
explanation.whyCorrect: The abbreviation 'del' indicates a deletion, and the first parentheses identify chromosome 4. The second parentheses give two band coordinates, p15.1 and p15.32, both on the p arm; two coordinates flanking a lost internal segment indicate an interstitial (not terminal) deletion of the short arm.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Both band coordinates are on the p (short) arm, and two coordinates indicate an interstitial rather than a terminal deletion.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A duplication would be abbreviated 'dup'; this is a 'del' indicating loss, not gain.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A translocation would be abbreviated 't' and involve two chromosomes; only chromosome 4 is affected here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The total count is 46 with no plus sign, so there is no extra whole chromosome 4.

questions.stem: The parents of the fetus with 46,XX,del(4)(p15.1p15.32) ask about the risk of recurrence in a future pregnancy. Chromosomal analysis of both parents is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate counseling?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The deletion is inherited from a parental balanced carrier, so recurrence risk is high

choices.label: B
choices.text: The deletion most likely arose spontaneously during gamete formation, and recurrence risk is low

choices.label: C
choices.text: The finding indicates a maternal Robertsonian translocation

choices.label: D
choices.text: The result reflects trisomy 4 and warrants testing for advanced maternal age

choices.label: E
choices.text: The deletion guarantees an identical deletion in all future pregnancies

explanation.summary: With normal parental karyotypes, the deletion is a de novo event that occurred during gamete formation, so recurrence risk is low.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because chromosomal evaluation of both parents is normal, neither carries a rearrangement that could be transmitted. This indicates the deletion happened spontaneously (de novo) during gamete formation in this pregnancy, and the recurrence risk in future pregnancies is low.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A high recurrence risk would apply if a parent were a balanced carrier, but both parental karyotypes are normal.
whyWrongByChoice.C: There is no Robertsonian translocation; the finding is an interstitial 4p deletion and the parents are normal.
whyWrongByChoice.D: This is a deletion (loss), not trisomy 4, and the mother is 26 rather than of advanced age.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A de novo event does not guarantee recurrence; the risk is actually low.

questions.stem: A karyotype is reported as 47,XY,+21. Which of the following best describes the abnormality indicated by this notation?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A balanced translocation between chromosomes 21 and Y

choices.label: D
choices.text: Trisomy 21 due to an extra copy of chromosome 21

choices.label: E
choices.text: A duplication of the long arm of chromosome 21

explanation.summary: A total of 47 chromosomes with '+21' denotes an extra whole chromosome 21, that is, trisomy 21.
explanation.whyCorrect: In aneuploidy notation the total chromosome number changes and a plus or minus sign designates the extra or missing chromosome. Here 47 chromosomes with +21 means there are three copies of chromosome 21, a trisomy, which is the most common clinically significant aneuploidy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Monosomy is loss of a copy, which would lower the count and use a minus sign, not +21.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A translocation is denoted 't', and the total here is 47 from a gained chromosome, not an exchange.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A deletion is denoted 'del' and would not raise the total chromosome number to 47.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A duplication is denoted 'dup' and affects part of an arm; this notation shows an extra whole chromosome.

questions.stem: A karyotype is reported as 46,XY,dup(5)(q31q42). Which statement best describes the genetic consequence of this finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It causes a partial trisomy from an extra copy of the q31 to q42 segment

choices.label: B
choices.text: It results in a net loss of the q31 to q42 segment

choices.label: D
choices.text: It inverts the orientation of chromosome 5 with no change in copy number

choices.label: E
choices.text: It represents complete monosomy of chromosome 5

explanation.summary: A duplication is an extra copy of a chromosome portion, producing a partial trisomy of that segment.
explanation.whyCorrect: The abbreviation 'dup' indicates a duplication, an abnormal extra copy of a portion of a chromosome. Here the segment q31 to q42 on the long arm of chromosome 5 is present in an extra copy, creating a situation of partial trisomy for that region.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A net loss would be a deletion ('del'), the opposite of a duplication.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Fusion of acrocentric chromosomes is a Robertsonian translocation, not a duplication.
whyWrongByChoice.D: An inversion flips a segment without changing copy number; a duplication adds a copy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Monosomy is loss of a whole chromosome; a duplication adds material to one chromosome.

questions.stem: A histology instructor asks about the basic repeating unit that packages DNA into chromatin. Which of the following correctly describes the protein core of a nucleosome?

choices.label: B
choices.text: An octamer of two copies each of H2A, H2B, H3, and H4

choices.label: E
choices.text: A supercoiled loop of ribosomal RNA

explanation.summary: The nucleosome core is a histone octamer of two copies each of H2A, H2B, H3, and H4, around which DNA is wound.
explanation.whyCorrect: Each chromosome is a continuous DNA double helix wound around histones and packaged as chromatin. The nucleosome, the basic repeating unit, consists of DNA wrapped around a core of two copies each of the histones H2A, H2B, H3, and H4.

whyWrongByChoice.A: H1 is the linker histone that sits between nucleosomes to compact chromatin further; it is not the core octamer.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cohesins hold sister chromatids together and are not part of the nucleosome core.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Spindle attachment occurs at the centromere and is unrelated to the nucleosome core proteins.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ribosomal RNA genes reside on acrocentric short arms and do not form the nucleosome core.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman delivers a newborn with flat facies, prominent epicanthal folds, a single transverse palmar crease, and a wide gap between the first and second toes. Karyotype shows 47,XX,+21. This trisomy most commonly results from nondisjunction occurring during which of the following?

explanation.summary: Most trisomy 21 arises from meiotic nondisjunction, the great majority in maternal meiosis I.
explanation.whyCorrect: About 95% of Down syndrome results from meiotic nondisjunction, with roughly 88% of those events occurring in female (maternal) meiosis I. This relates to oogenesis, in which oogonia remain arrested in prophase I for many years, and the risk rises with advanced maternal age.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Maternal errors more often occur in meiosis I than meiosis II; meiosis II is a less common source.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Paternal nondisjunction accounts for only about 8% of cases, far fewer than maternal errors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Paternal meiosis II is an even rarer contributor than paternal meiosis I.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mitotic nondisjunction after fertilization produces mosaicism (46,XX/47,XX,+21), not the uniform 47,XX,+21 seen here.

questions.topicName: Numerical & Structural Chromosomal Alterations (1/3) - Variants in Chromosome Number

questions.stem: A newborn is noted to have a punched-out scalp lesion (cutis aplasia), microcephaly, midline cleft lip and palate, extra digits on both hands, and imaging shows a single fused cerebral ventricle. Which karyotype is most consistent with this presentation?

explanation.summary: Cutis aplasia, holoprosencephaly, cleft lip/palate, and polydactyly are hallmarks of Patau syndrome (trisomy 13).
explanation.whyCorrect: Patau syndrome (trisomy 13, 47,+13) is characterized by cutis aplasia, microcephaly, holoprosencephaly, microphthalmia, cleft lip/palate, polydactyly, umbilical hernia/omphalocele, and rocker-bottom feet, with congenital heart and renal defects. Cutis aplasia in particular is specific for Patau syndrome.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Trisomy 18 (Edwards) features micrognathia, clenched hands with overlapping fingers, and rocker-bottom feet rather than cutis aplasia and polydactyly.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Trisomy 21 (Down) presents with flat facies, single palmar crease, and a toe gap, not holoprosencephaly and cutis aplasia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 45,X (Turner) causes short stature and a webbed neck, not this constellation of midline and scalp defects.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 47,XXY (Klinefelter) is a sex-chromosome aneuploidy and does not produce these severe congenital malformations.

questions.topicName: Numerical & Structural Chromosomal Alterations (1/3) - Variants in Chromosome Number

questions.stem: A neonate is born with micrognathia, a prominent occiput, low-set ears, clenched fists with the second and fifth fingers overlapping the third and fourth, and rocker-bottom feet. A ventricular septal defect is detected. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Clenched hands with overlapping fingers plus micrognathia and rocker-bottom feet point to trisomy 18.
explanation.whyCorrect: Edwards syndrome (trisomy 18) classically presents with micrognathia, prominent occiput, low-set ears, clenched hands with overlapping fingers, rocker-bottom feet, and limited hip adduction, along with congenital heart and renal defects. Prognosis is poor, with 50% dying by 2 months and 90-95% by 1 year.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Down syndrome shows flat facies, single palmar crease, and a toe gap, not overlapping fingers and micrognathia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Patau syndrome features cutis aplasia, holoprosencephaly, cleft lip/palate, and polydactyly.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Turner syndrome (45,X) causes short stature and webbed neck, not this pattern of malformations.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Klinefelter syndrome is a sex-chromosome aneuploidy without these congenital anomalies.

questions.topicName: Numerical & Structural Chromosomal Alterations (1/3) - Variants in Chromosome Number

questions.stem: A newborn with flat facies, upslanting palpebral fissures, and a single palmar crease develops bilious vomiting on the first day of life. An abdominal radiograph shows a 'double bubble' sign. Which of the following gastrointestinal anomalies is most strongly associated with this infant's underlying chromosomal disorder?

explanation.summary: Down syndrome is associated with duodenal atresia, which produces the double-bubble sign.
explanation.whyCorrect: The dysmorphic features indicate Down syndrome (trisomy 21). The brick lists duodenal atresia and Hirschsprung disease among its gastrointestinal associations; duodenal atresia classically causes bilious vomiting and a double-bubble sign on radiograph.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Tracheoesophageal fistula is not among the associations emphasized for trisomy 21 and does not produce a double-bubble sign.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pyloric stenosis causes non-bilious projectile vomiting and is not a Down syndrome association here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Meckel diverticulum typically presents with painless lower GI bleeding, not neonatal bilious vomiting.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Biliary atresia presents with progressive neonatal jaundice, not a double-bubble sign.

questions.topicName: Numerical & Structural Chromosomal Alterations (1/3) - Variants in Chromosome Number

questions.stem: A phenotypically normal woman has the karyotype 45,XX,rob(21;21), a balanced Robertsonian translocation in which both copies of chromosome 21 are fused into a single chromosome. Regarding her potential offspring, which of the following is the most accurate counseling statement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Every liveborn child she has will have Down syndrome

choices.label: B
choices.text: About one half of her liveborn children will be chromosomally normal

choices.label: C
choices.text: One sixth of her liveborn children will be balanced translocation carriers

choices.label: D
choices.text: She cannot achieve any viable pregnancy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Her children are at increased risk for Patau syndrome

explanation.summary: In a rob(21;21) carrier, viable gametes yield only trisomy 21, so every surviving child has Down syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because the long arms of both chromosome 21s are physically attached, they cannot segregate away from each other during meiosis. Half of the gametes carry the fused 21q21q chromosome (leading to trisomy 21 on fertilization) and half carry no chromosome 21 (leading to nonviable monosomy 21). Thus, every liveborn child will have Down syndrome.

whyWrongByChoice.B: No gamete can carry exactly one normal chromosome 21, so a chromosomally normal child is not possible.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A balanced carrier outcome is impossible because the single fused chromosome cannot recreate a normal balanced complement in offspring.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Viable pregnancy is possible; the trisomy 21 (Down syndrome) conceptions are viable.
whyWrongByChoice.E: This translocation involves chromosome 21, producing trisomy 21 (Down), not trisomy 13 (Patau).

questions.topicName: Numerical & Structural Chromosomal Alterations (1/3) - Variants in Chromosome Number

questions.stem: A healthy 34-year-old woman has had four miscarriages, and two of her fetuses had translocation trisomy 14. Her karyotype is 45,XX,rob(13;14). Which of the following best explains why she is a healthy carrier yet has difficulty carrying a pregnancy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: She is a balanced carrier, but abnormal segregation produces unbalanced gametes

choices.label: B
choices.text: She has mosaic trisomy 14 confined to her ovaries

choices.label: C
choices.text: She lost critical genetic material when the translocation formed

choices.label: D
choices.text: Her advanced maternal age causes meiosis II nondisjunction

choices.label: E
choices.text: She carries a point mutation that is lethal in the homozygous state

explanation.summary: A balanced Robertsonian carrier is healthy but produces unbalanced gametes during meiotic segregation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Balanced translocation carriers are phenotypically normal because there is no net loss or gain of critical genetic material. However, the rearrangement causes problems during gamete production: the fused chromosome cannot pair and segregate normally, so a large fraction of gametes are unbalanced, leading to nonviable fetuses or trisomic offspring.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Her karyotype reflects a constitutional balanced translocation, not confined ovarian mosaicism for trisomy 14.
whyWrongByChoice.C: By definition a balanced translocation involves no loss or gain of critical genetic information, which is why she is healthy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Her losses are driven by unbalanced segregation of the translocation, not by age-related meiosis II nondisjunction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The problem is a chromosomal rearrangement affecting dosage, not a single-gene point mutation.

questions.topicName: Numerical & Structural Chromosomal Alterations (1/3) - Variants in Chromosome Number

questions.stem: A 3-year-old girl has milder-than-expected features of Down syndrome. Karyotype analysis of a blood sample reports 46,XX/47,XX,+21. Which of the following mechanisms best explains this finding?

explanation.summary: Two cell lines (46 and 47,+21) indicate mosaicism from a postfertilization mitotic error.
explanation.whyCorrect: The notation 46,XX/47,XX,+21 denotes two genetically distinct cell lineages derived from a single zygote (mosaicism). This does not occur during gamete production but is a postfertilization error in mitosis; mitotic nondisjunction creates a clonal population of aneuploid cells so that only some cells carry the extra chromosome 21.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Maternal meiosis I nondisjunction produces a uniform 47,XX,+21 in every cell, not two cell lines.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Paternal meiosis II nondisjunction likewise affects every cell of the zygote, not a mosaic subset.
whyWrongByChoice.D: An unbalanced Robertsonian translocation would appear as a rob(...) karyotype with +21, not two separate cell lines.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Uniparental disomy does not change chromosome number and would not produce a 46/47 mosaic pattern.

questions.topicName: Numerical & Structural Chromosomal Alterations (1/3) - Variants in Chromosome Number

questions.stem: A 46-year-old man with Down syndrome is brought in with a two-year history of progressive memory loss and functional decline consistent with early-onset Alzheimer disease. Increased dosage of a gene on which chromosome best explains his elevated risk?

explanation.summary: Chromosome 21 carries APP, so trisomy 21 gives three copies and raises Alzheimer risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: Chromosome 21 carries the gene coding for amyloid precursor protein (APP), which is involved in the pathophysiology of Alzheimer disease. Three copies of APP in Down syndrome are thought to increase Alzheimer risk, with disease onset typically beginning around age 40.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Chromosome 18 is implicated in Edwards syndrome, not the APP-driven Alzheimer association.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chromosome 13 is implicated in Patau syndrome and does not carry APP.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Chromosome 14 is commonly involved in Robertsonian translocations but does not explain the APP dosage effect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The APP gene relevant to this association is on chromosome 21, not the X chromosome.

questions.topicName: Numerical & Structural Chromosomal Alterations (1/3) - Variants in Chromosome Number

questions.stem: A 44-year-old pregnant woman is counseled that her risk of having a fetus with trisomy 21 has risen to roughly 1 in 25, compared with about 1 in 1500 in women younger than 20. Which feature of female gametogenesis best accounts for this age-related increase?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Oocytes remain arrested in prophase I for decades

choices.label: B
choices.text: Spermatogonia divide continuously throughout adult life

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sister chromatids are duplicated during fertilization

choices.label: E
choices.text: Mitotic nondisjunction increases in the endometrium with age

explanation.summary: Prolonged meiotic arrest of oocytes underlies the maternal-age effect on nondisjunction.
explanation.whyCorrect: In oogenesis, oogonia remain arrested in prophase I for many years. This prolonged arrest predisposes to nondisjunction, and the risk increases with advanced maternal age (from about 1:1500 in women under 20 to about 1:25 in women over 45). Most Down syndrome nondisjunction occurs in maternal meiosis I.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Continuous spermatogonial division relates to paternal factors and does not explain the maternal-age effect on trisomy 21.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Oocytes do not complete meiosis before birth; they are held in prolonged arrest, which is the point of vulnerability.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sister chromatids are not duplicated at fertilization; DNA replication precedes meiosis in the oocyte.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The relevant error is meiotic nondisjunction in the oocyte, not endometrial mitotic nondisjunction.

questions.topicName: Numerical & Structural Chromosomal Alterations (1/3) - Variants in Chromosome Number

questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy with Down syndrome develops fatigue, easy bruising, and pancytopenia, and a marrow examination confirms an acute leukemia. Which type of leukemia is most characteristically associated with his chromosomal disorder?

explanation.summary: Down syndrome carries an increased risk of leukemia, usually acute myeloid leukemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick lists an increased risk of leukemia (usually AML) among the associations of trisomy 21, and notes this is thought to be due to silencing of PRC2. Acute myeloid leukemia is therefore the characteristic association in a young child with Down syndrome.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Chronic myeloid leukemia is associated with the BCR-ABL translocation, not specifically with Down syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chronic lymphocytic leukemia is a disease of older adults and is not the Down syndrome association.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hairy cell leukemia is a rare B-cell malignancy of adults, unrelated to trisomy 21.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Adult T-cell leukemia is linked to HTLV-1 infection, not to Down syndrome.

questions.topicName: Numerical & Structural Chromosomal Alterations (1/3) - Variants in Chromosome Number

questions.stem: A first-trimester miscarriage is evaluated, and cytogenetic analysis of the products of conception reveals 69,XXY. Which of the following terms best describes this chromosome complement?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Aneuploid due to a single extra chromosome

explanation.summary: 69 chromosomes is an exact multiple of the haploid number (3n), making it triploid and euploid.
explanation.whyCorrect: When the chromosome number is an exact multiple of the haploid number (n = 23), it is euploid. 69 chromosomes equals 3n (triploid), which is euploid but not viable; triploid and tetraploid complements are occasionally seen in fetuses but do not survive.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Aneuploidy refers to a number that is NOT an exact multiple of the haploid number; 69 is exactly 3n.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The count is 69 chromosomes (triploid), not a diploid 46 with a translocation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Monosomy means one missing chromosome (2n - 1); triploidy is a gain of an entire haploid set.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A single 69,XXY result reflects one triploid cell line, not a mosaic of two distinct lineages.

questions.topicName: Numerical & Structural Chromosomal Alterations (1/3) - Variants in Chromosome Number

questions.stem: A 15-year-old girl is evaluated for short stature and primary amenorrhea and is found to have a webbed neck and widely spaced nipples. Her karyotype shows a single sex chromosome. This condition is notable as which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The only monosomy compatible with life

choices.label: B
choices.text: The most common viable autosomal trisomy

choices.label: C
choices.text: The most commonly occurring trisomy overall

choices.label: D
choices.text: A result of an unbalanced Robertsonian translocation

choices.label: E
choices.text: A euploid condition with a normal chromosome count

explanation.summary: Monosomy X (Turner syndrome) is the only monosomy compatible with life.
explanation.whyCorrect: The findings describe Turner syndrome (45,X). Autosomal monosomies are always incompatible with life; the only monosomy compatible with life is monosomy of the sex chromosome X, which causes Turner syndrome.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The most common viable autosomal trisomy is Down syndrome (trisomy 21), not a monosomy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Trisomy 16 is the most commonly occurring trisomy overall, but it is always fatal in utero and is not this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Turner syndrome results from loss of an X chromosome, not from an unbalanced Robertsonian translocation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 45,X is aneuploid (2n - 1), not euploid with a normal chromosome count.

questions.topicName: Numerical & Structural Chromosomal Alterations (1/3) - Variants in Chromosome Number

questions.stem: A 16-year-old girl presents for evaluation of primary amenorrhea. She is embarrassed that she has not started menstruating like her friends. On examination her height is at the fifth percentile for age, and you note extra skin folds on the neck, a broad chest, and a systolic murmur in the aortic region. Which of the following karyotypes is most likely?

explanation.summary: Short stature, webbed neck, broad chest, and primary amenorrhea in a phenotypic female indicate Turner syndrome (45,X).
explanation.whyCorrect: Turner syndrome results from a single X chromosome (45,X, also written 45,XO). It is the most common cause of primary amenorrhea because the ovaries are replaced by nonfunctional fibrous tissue, and it classically produces short stature, a short webbed neck, a broad chest with widely spaced nipples, and cardiac defects such as a bicuspid aortic valve.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A 46,XY karyotype is a normal male and would not present as a phenotypic female with primary amenorrhea and these somatic features.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Triple X (47,XXX) females typically have normal sexual development, normal fertility, and are often taller than average, not short with amenorrhea.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY) occurs in males, who present with tall stature, small testes, and gynecomastia rather than these findings.
whyWrongByChoice.E: XYY (47,XYY) occurs in males with normal genitalia and tall stature, not in a phenotypic female with amenorrhea.

questions.stem: You are counseling the parents of a newborn girl whose karyotype is reported as 45,X. They ask what cardiac problem she is most at risk for. Which of the following congenital heart defects is most strongly associated with her diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Bicuspid aortic valve is the most common cardiac malformation in Turner syndrome (45,X).
explanation.whyCorrect: Cardiovascular disease is the most serious health problem in Turner syndrome. Aortic valve abnormalities, primarily a bicuspid aortic valve, along with aortic coarctation, are the most common cardiac malformations and contribute to reduced life expectancy through aortic dilation and dissection.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Atrial septal defect is characteristically associated with Down syndrome, not Turner syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Tetralogy of Fallot is classically linked to DiGeorge (22q11.2 deletion) syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Tricuspid valve atresia is associated with lithium exposure during pregnancy, not with monosomy X.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ventricular septal defect is characteristically associated with Down syndrome rather than Turner syndrome.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man is evaluated for infertility. He is tall with disproportionately long arms and legs, and examination reveals gynecomastia, sparse facial hair, and small, firm testes. Semen analysis shows azoospermia. Which of the following karyotypes is most likely?

explanation.summary: Tall stature, gynecomastia, small firm testes, and infertility in a male indicate Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY).
explanation.whyCorrect: Klinefelter syndrome results from an extra X chromosome (47,XXY). The Y chromosome drives male differentiation while the extra X imparts some feminizing features; patients characteristically have a tall body with long limbs, gynecomastia, small fibrotic testes, reduced testosterone, and infertility from germ cell degeneration.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Turner syndrome (45,X) occurs in phenotypic females and presents with short stature and ovarian failure.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A normal 46,XY male would not have gynecomastia, small fibrotic testes, and azoospermia as a syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Triple X (47,XXX) occurs in females, not males.
whyWrongByChoice.E: XYY (47,XYY) males have normal genitalia, normal testosterone, and normal fertility, not gynecomastia and azoospermia.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with a 47,XXY karyotype is being counseled about hormone therapy. His small, fibrotic testes show germ cell degeneration on biopsy. Which of the following hormonal findings is most consistent with his diagnosis, and best explains why testosterone supplementation is often prescribed?

explanation.summary: Klinefelter syndrome causes reduced testosterone from testicular dysfunction, so testosterone supplementation is commonly given.
explanation.whyCorrect: In Klinefelter syndrome the testes are small and fibrotic with germ cell degeneration, leading to reduced testosterone production and a testosterone-to-estrogen imbalance. Because of this deficiency, testosterone supplementation is often prescribed to address the hypogonadal state.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Testosterone production is reduced, not increased, in Klinefelter syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Estrogen is not absent; in fact a relative testosterone-to-estrogen imbalance contributes to features such as gynecomastia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Elevated progesterone is not a described feature of Klinefelter syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Gonadal hormone balance is abnormal, which is precisely why supplementation is needed.

questions.stem: A 15-year-old boy is referred for behavioral concerns including impulsivity and difficulty focusing. He is notably tall for his family, has severe acne, and is noted to have flat feet and mild tremor. Genital examination is normal, and serum testosterone is within the normal range. Which of the following karyotypes is most likely?

explanation.summary: Tall stature, severe acne, flat feet, normal genitalia, and behavioral impulsivity with normal testosterone indicate XYY (Jacobs) syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: XYY syndrome (47,XYY) occurs in males who have normal male external genitalia and normal testosterone levels. Associated findings include tall stature, hypotonia with flat feet (pes planus) and tremor, severe acne in adolescence, and behavioral features such as impulsivity, ADHD, and autism spectrum disorders.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Turner syndrome (45,X) occurs in short-statured females, not tall males with normal genitalia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A normal 46,XY male would not constitute the described cluster of tall stature with behavioral and skeletal findings as a syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Triple X (47,XXX) occurs in females.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY) presents with small testes, gynecomastia, and low testosterone, not normal genitalia and normal testosterone.

questions.stem: An 18-year-old woman is found to have a sex chromosome aneuploidy after genetic testing during a learning-disability evaluation. She is taller than average, has normal external genitalia and normal pubertal development, and is expected to have normal fertility. Which of the following karyotypes best fits this presentation?

explanation.summary: A taller-than-average female with normal genitalia, normal fertility, and learning-disability risk fits triple X syndrome (47,XXX).
explanation.whyCorrect: Triple X syndrome (47,XXX) affects females and may cause minimal or no noticeable effects. External genitalia and sexual development are normal, affected individuals are often taller than average and can have children, and the main associated finding is an increased risk of learning disabilities.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Turner syndrome (45,X) causes short stature, ovarian failure, and infertility, the opposite of this picture.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A 46,XX karyotype is a normal female and is not a sex chromosome aneuploidy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY) occurs in males with small testes and infertility.
whyWrongByChoice.E: XYY (47,XYY) occurs in males, not females.

questions.stem: During early embryonic development, a transcription factor encoded by a single gene commits the bipotential gonad toward testis formation; embryos lacking this gene tend to develop female sexual organs. On which chromosome is this gene normally located, and what is its product?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The Y chromosome; it encodes the testis-determining transcription factor

choices.label: B
choices.text: The X chromosome; it encodes an X-inactivation transcript

choices.label: C
choices.text: An autosome; it encodes a structural collagen protein

choices.label: D
choices.text: The pseudoautosomal region; it encodes a recombination enzyme

choices.label: E
choices.text: The mitochondrial genome; it encodes an oxidative enzyme

explanation.summary: The SRY gene on the Y chromosome encodes testis-determining factor, a transcription factor that directs male sex determination.
explanation.whyCorrect: SRY is normally found on the Y chromosome and encodes testis-determining factor (TDF), a transcription factor critical for sex determination. Embryos with a copy of SRY tend to develop male sexual organs, and those without it tend to develop female sexual organs.

whyWrongByChoice.B: SRY is not on the X chromosome and does not encode an X-inactivation transcript; X-inactivation is a separate dosage-compensation process.
whyWrongByChoice.C: SRY is not autosomal and does not encode a structural collagen protein; it encodes a transcription factor.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The pseudoautosomal region allows X-Y pairing and recombination but does not itself contain the SRY gene.
whyWrongByChoice.E: SRY is a nuclear sex-chromosome gene, not a mitochondrial gene, and its product is a transcription factor, not an oxidative enzyme.

questions.stem: A researcher is studying how paternal meiotic errors generate abnormal sex chromosome complements. If nondisjunction occurs during male meiosis II and the resulting abnormal sperm fertilizes a normal X-bearing egg, which of the following karyotypes is most characteristically produced?

explanation.summary: Paternal meiosis II nondisjunction can create a YY sperm that, with a normal X egg, yields 47,XYY.
explanation.whyCorrect: Nondisjunction during male meiosis II fails to separate sister chromatids and can generate XX or YY sperm. A YY sperm fertilizing a normal X egg produces a 47,XYY zygote. In contrast, meiosis I nondisjunction is what generates XY sperm.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 45,X arises when a nullisomic gamete lacking any sex chromosome combines with an X gamete, not specifically from a meiosis II YY sperm.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 47,XXX requires an XX egg fertilized by an X sperm; it is a maternal, not this paternal meiosis II, outcome.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 47,XXY arises from an XY sperm (a meiosis I error) plus an X egg, or an XX egg plus a Y sperm, not from a meiosis II YY sperm and an X egg.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 47,YYY would require multiple abnormal events and complete absence of an X, which cannot result from a single YY sperm fertilizing a normal X egg.

questions.stem: In a pregnancy, a nondisjunction event occurs during maternal meiosis while paternal spermatogenesis is entirely normal. Which of the following offspring karyotypes is LEAST likely to result from this pregnancy?

explanation.summary: Because the mother is XX, maternal nondisjunction can only make XX or nullisomic eggs, so a 47,XYY child is essentially impossible without a paternal error.
explanation.whyCorrect: The maternal genotype is XX, so nondisjunction during maternal meiosis can produce only XX eggs or eggs lacking any sex chromosome. Generating 47,XYY requires two Y chromosomes, which the mother cannot supply; with normal paternal sperm, XYY is essentially not achievable.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 45,X can occur if a maternal egg lacking a sex chromosome is fertilized by a normal X sperm.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 47,XXX can occur if a maternal XX egg is fertilized by a normal X sperm.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 47,XXY can occur if a maternal XX egg is fertilized by a normal Y sperm.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 46,XX is a normal outcome when a normal maternal X egg meets a normal X sperm.

questions.stem: Although the X and Y chromosomes are structurally very different, they must pair and can undergo limited homologous recombination during male meiosis. Which feature of the sex chromosomes makes this pairing possible?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The pseudoautosomal region shared by X and Y

choices.label: B
choices.text: The SRY gene on the Y chromosome

choices.label: D
choices.text: The large number of unique genes on the X chromosome

choices.label: E
choices.text: The centromeric heterochromatin of the Y chromosome

explanation.summary: The pseudoautosomal region, essentially identical on X and Y, allows the two to pair and recombine like autosomes during male meiosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Genes in the pseudoautosomal region are found on both the X and Y chromosomes, and these stretches are essentially identical. This homology lets the X and Y pair and undergo homologous recombination during male meiosis, similar to how autosomes behave.

whyWrongByChoice.B: SRY drives male sex determination but does not mediate the physical pairing of X and Y during meiosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: X-inactivation is a dosage-compensation mechanism in cells with extra X chromosomes and is unrelated to meiotic pairing.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The many unique X genes are precisely the non-homologous portion and do not enable X-Y pairing.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Centromeric heterochromatin of the Y is not the homologous region that permits X-Y pairing and recombination.

questions.stem: A geneticist explains why individuals with two or more X chromosomes do not express X-linked genes at proportionally higher levels than 46,XY males. Which of the following processes accounts for this normalization of X-gene dosage?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Deletion of the extra X chromosome

explanation.summary: X-inactivation silences extra X chromosomes so that X-linked gene dosage is balanced between the sexes.
explanation.whyCorrect: In individuals with two or more X chromosomes, inactivation of the extra X chromosomes is a normal physiological process. It ensures that X-chromosomal genes are not expressed at proportionally higher levels compared with XY males, providing dosage compensation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Nondisjunction is a cause of aneuploidy, not a mechanism for balancing gene dosage.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pseudoautosomal recombination allows X-Y pairing in meiosis but does not silence extra X genes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: SRY expression governs male sex determination and does not regulate X-gene dosage compensation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The extra X chromosome is not physically deleted; it is transcriptionally inactivated while remaining present.

questions.stem: A 17-year-old girl with Turner syndrome (45,X) has never menstruated. Her mother asks why she has not begun having periods. Which of the following best explains the underlying cause of her primary amenorrhea?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ovarian dysgenesis with premature ovarian failure and low estrogen

choices.label: B
choices.text: An imperforate hymen obstructing menstrual outflow

choices.label: C
choices.text: Excess testosterone from functioning testicular tissue

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pituitary overproduction of estrogen and progesterone

choices.label: E
choices.text: Uterine agenesis with normally functioning ovaries

explanation.summary: Turner syndrome causes ovarian dysgenesis and premature ovarian failure with insufficient estrogen, making it the most common cause of primary amenorrhea.
explanation.whyCorrect: In Turner syndrome the ovaries are replaced by nonfunctional fibrous tissue and stop functioning at a young age (premature ovarian failure). The resulting lack of estrogen means menstruation and pubertal development do not occur normally, and this ovarian dysgenesis makes Turner syndrome the most common cause of primary amenorrhea.

whyWrongByChoice.B: An imperforate hymen causes outflow obstruction, not the ovarian failure and low estrogen characteristic of Turner syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Turner patients do not have functioning testicular tissue or excess testosterone; the problem is estrogen deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The hormonal picture is estrogen deficiency, not pituitary overproduction of estrogen and progesterone.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The defect is ovarian (dysgenesis), not uterine agenesis with normal ovaries.

questions.stem: A 1-month-old girl is evaluated for microcephaly and low birth weight noted at birth. Over the first few weeks of life she has had difficulty feeding because of trouble swallowing. Her mother reports a distinctive high-pitched, cat-like cry that was very different from that of her older sibling. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Microcephaly, intellectual disability, and a high-pitched cat-like cry in an infant indicate cri du chat syndrome (5p deletion).
explanation.whyCorrect: Cri du chat syndrome results from a deletion on the short arm of chromosome 5 (5p-). The name means 'cry of the cat' in French and refers to the characteristic high-pitched cry; other features include microcephaly, intellectual disability, and cardiac abnormalities such as ventricular septal defects.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Williams syndrome (7q11.23 deletion) presents with elfin facies, overfriendliness, and supravalvular aortic stenosis, not a cat-like cry.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 22q11.2 deletion syndrome features conotruncal cardiac defects, immunodeficiency, and hypocalcemia rather than a high-pitched cry.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Down syndrome (trisomy 21) is associated with flat epicanthal folds and a single palmar crease, not the described cry.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Turner syndrome (45,X) affects females with short stature and gonadal dysgenesis and is not characterized by a cat-like cry.

questions.topicName: Numerical and Structural Chromosomal Alterations (2/3) - Subchromosomal Deletion Syndromes

questions.stem: A geneticist confirms cri du chat syndrome in a female infant. Which of the following karyotype descriptions is most consistent with this diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Cri du chat syndrome is caused by a deletion of the short arm of chromosome 5, written as 46,XX,del(5p) in a female.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cri du chat syndrome is caused by a deletion on chromosome 5p. Because the breakpoints and extent of the deleted segment are variable, the notation is often written simply as del(5p), with a band location added only when known.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 47,XXY indicates a male with Klinefelter syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 46,XX,del(22q11.2) describes a female with 22q11.2 deletion (DiGeorge) syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 45,X indicates a female with Turner syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 46,XX,del(7q11.23) describes a female with Williams syndrome.

questions.topicName: Numerical and Structural Chromosomal Alterations (2/3) - Subchromosomal Deletion Syndromes

questions.stem: A newborn boy has a truncus arteriosus detected on echocardiography, a cleft palate, and an unusual facial appearance. On the second day of life he develops jitteriness and a seizure; laboratory studies reveal a low serum calcium concentration. Deletion of which of the following chromosomal regions is the most likely cause?

explanation.summary: Conotruncal cardiac defects with hypocalcemia and craniofacial anomalies point to a 22q11.2 deletion.
explanation.whyCorrect: 22q11.2 deletion syndrome (DiGeorge/velocardiofacial syndrome) results from a ~3 Mb deletion on chromosome 22 and is characterized by craniofacial anomalies, conotruncal cardiac defects, immunodeficiency, and hypocalcemia. Hypocalcemia and immunodeficiency reflect underdevelopment of the parathyroid glands and thymus.

whyWrongByChoice.B: 7q11.23 deletion causes Williams syndrome, which features supravalvular aortic stenosis and hypercalcemia, not hypocalcemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 5p deletion causes cri du chat syndrome, characterized by a high-pitched cry and microcephaly.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 15q11 abnormalities are linked to Prader-Willi/Angelman syndromes, not the conotruncal-defect/hypocalcemia phenotype described.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 4p16 deletion is associated with Wolf-Hirschhorn syndrome and is not the cause of this presentation.

questions.topicName: Numerical and Structural Chromosomal Alterations (2/3) - Subchromosomal Deletion Syndromes

questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy is described by his parents as strikingly friendly with strangers. On examination he has a broad forehead, puffiness around the eyes, a flat nasal bridge, full cheeks, and a small chin. A systolic murmur is heard, and echocardiography reveals supravalvular aortic stenosis. Deletion of which chromosomal region best explains these findings?

explanation.summary: Elfin facies, overfriendliness, and supravalvular aortic stenosis indicate Williams syndrome (7q11.23 deletion).
explanation.whyCorrect: Williams syndrome results from a deletion at 7q11.23 affecting 25-27 genes. It is characterized by mild to moderate intellectual disability, distinctive 'elf-like' facial features, overfriendliness with a lack of social inhibition, hypercalcemia, and cardiovascular disease, most commonly supravalvular aortic stenosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: 22q11.2 deletion is associated with conotruncal cardiac defects and hypocalcemia, not supravalvular aortic stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 5p deletion causes cri du chat syndrome, with microcephaly and a cat-like cry rather than these features.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Xp22 deletions are not the cause of the described Williams-syndrome phenotype.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 17p11.2 abnormalities relate to other genomic syndromes and do not explain supravalvular aortic stenosis with overfriendliness.

questions.topicName: Numerical and Structural Chromosomal Alterations (2/3) - Subchromosomal Deletion Syndromes

questions.stem: Two unrelated patients are found to have deletions of nearly identical size and genomic location. This kind of recurrent structural variant is best explained by which of the following mechanisms?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nonhomologous end joining after a double-stranded break

choices.label: C
choices.text: A single point mutation in a coding exon

explanation.summary: Recurrent structural variants arise from nonallelic homologous recombination, producing consistent size and location across unrelated people.
explanation.whyCorrect: Recurrent structural variants are caused by nonallelic homologous recombination, unequal crossing over between misaligned chromatids or chromosomes that contain highly homologous sequences. Because these homologous stretches sit at consistent genomic sites, the resulting rearrangements have similar size and location in unrelated individuals.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Nonhomologous end joining produces nonrecurrent variants with highly variable breakpoints, not recurrent ones.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A point mutation alters a single base and does not delete a multi-gene chromosomal segment.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Trinucleotide repeat expansion causes disorders like Huntington disease, not recurrent segmental deletions.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Uniparental disomy involves inheriting both homologs from one parent and does not create these recurrent deletions.

questions.topicName: Numerical and Structural Chromosomal Alterations (2/3) - Subchromosomal Deletion Syndromes

questions.stem: A structural variant is described as nonrecurrent, with breakpoints and deleted-segment size that differ from patient to patient and appear to occur at random. Which repair pathway is most likely responsible for this type of variant?

explanation.summary: Nonrecurrent variants result from nonhomologous end joining, an error-prone repair of double-stranded breaks with variable breakpoints.
explanation.whyCorrect: Nonrecurrent structural variants are caused by nonhomologous end joining (NHEJ), the pathway that repairs double-stranded DNA breaks. Because NHEJ does not use a homologous template, it is inherently error-prone and can occur at random, giving rise to highly variable breakpoints.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Nonallelic homologous recombination produces recurrent variants of consistent size and location, the opposite of what is described.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mismatch repair corrects base-pairing errors from replication and does not generate large deletions.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Base excision repair removes damaged single bases and is unrelated to double-strand-break rejoining.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nucleotide excision repair removes bulky lesions such as pyrimidine dimers, not chromosomal breakpoints.

questions.topicName: Numerical and Structural Chromosomal Alterations (2/3) - Subchromosomal Deletion Syndromes

questions.stem: A 6-year-old girl is seen in the outpatient pediatrics clinic for follow-up, and the geneticist reports a 7q11.23 chromosomal microdeletion. Which of the following findings would you most expect to observe on examination?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Flat epicanthal folds and a single palmar crease

choices.label: D
choices.text: Many bruises, low-grade fever, and weight loss

explanation.summary: A 7q11.23 microdeletion causes Williams syndrome, with elfin facies and an overfriendly personality.
explanation.whyCorrect: Williams syndrome is caused by a microdeletion on chromosome 7 (7q11.23). Its features include distinctive 'elf-like' facial features, mild to moderate intellectual disability, and a personality notable for overfriendliness and lack of social inhibition.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Flat epicanthal folds and a single palmar crease are characteristic of Down syndrome (trisomy 21).
whyWrongByChoice.C: Frequent infections and malformed thumbs are seen in Fanconi anemia, a defect in nonhomologous end joining DNA repair.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bruising, fever, and weight loss are nonspecific features of leukemia, which can arise from a chromosomal translocation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Microcephaly and a high-pitched cry occur in cri du chat syndrome from a 5p deletion, not a 7q11.23 deletion.

questions.topicName: Numerical and Structural Chromosomal Alterations (2/3) - Subchromosomal Deletion Syndromes

questions.stem: A neonate with a 22q11.2 deletion is admitted with hypocalcemic tetany and is found to have severely reduced T-lymphocyte numbers. Underdevelopment of which structure best accounts for the immunodeficiency in this syndrome?

explanation.summary: In 22q11.2 deletion syndrome, thymic aplasia leads to T-cell immunodeficiency, and parathyroid hypoplasia causes hypocalcemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: 22q11.2 deletion syndrome (DiGeorge) is remembered by the features of cardiac defects, abnormal facies, thymic aplasia, cleft palate, and hypocalcemia. Failure of thymic development impairs T-lymphocyte maturation, producing the immunodeficiency, while parathyroid hypoplasia produces hypocalcemia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Bone marrow produces lymphoid precursors but is not the structure that fails in 22q11.2 deletion syndrome; the T-cell defect stems from thymic aplasia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The spleen contributes to immune function but its underdevelopment does not explain the T-cell deficiency of this syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Adrenal cortical dysfunction is not a feature of 22q11.2 deletion syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A bursa-equivalent controls B-cell development in birds; the T-cell immunodeficiency here is due to thymic, not B-cell, failure.

questions.topicName: Numerical and Structural Chromosomal Alterations (2/3) - Subchromosomal Deletion Syndromes

questions.stem: A pediatrician is considering both Williams syndrome and 22q11.2 deletion syndrome in a young child with dysmorphic features and a heart murmur. Which feature would most specifically favor Williams syndrome over 22q11.2 deletion syndrome?

explanation.summary: Supravalvular aortic stenosis plus an overfriendly personality distinguishes Williams syndrome from 22q11.2 deletion syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: Although the two syndromes share overlapping features and are both on the differential, the most common congenital heart lesion differs: supravalvular aortic stenosis is characteristic of Williams syndrome, whereas conotruncal malformations are associated with 22q11.2 deletion syndrome. Overfriendliness and excessive empathy are also unique to Williams syndrome.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Conotruncal malformations are associated with 22q11.2 deletion syndrome, not Williams syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hypocalcemia is a feature of 22q11.2 deletion syndrome; Williams syndrome is associated with hypercalcemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Immunodeficiency from thymic aplasia is a hallmark of 22q11.2 deletion syndrome, not Williams syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cleft palate is part of the 22q11.2 deletion (velocardiofacial) phenotype rather than Williams syndrome.

questions.topicName: Numerical and Structural Chromosomal Alterations (2/3) - Subchromosomal Deletion Syndromes

questions.stem: Which of the following statements best describes why some subchromosomal variants are classified as recurrent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They tend to occur at nearly the same location and size in unrelated patients

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are inherited in an autosomal dominant fashion in every affected family

choices.label: C
choices.text: They always involve the same single nucleotide

choices.label: D
choices.text: They arise only during mitosis in somatic cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: They have highly variable breakpoints that differ between patients

explanation.summary: Recurrent variants occur at nearly the same genomic location and size in unrelated individuals because they arise from consistently placed homologous sequences.
explanation.whyCorrect: Recurrent variants occur in nearly the same location and size in unrelated patients. This consistency reflects nonallelic homologous recombination between highly similar stretches of DNA that lie at fixed genomic positions.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Recurrence refers to consistent genomic location across unrelated people, not a particular inheritance pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Recurrent structural variants involve segments of DNA, not a single nucleotide.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The recurrent mechanism relates to meiotic nonallelic homologous recombination, not exclusively somatic mitosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Highly variable breakpoints describe nonrecurrent variants caused by nonhomologous end joining.

questions.topicName: Numerical and Structural Chromosomal Alterations (2/3) - Subchromosomal Deletion Syndromes

questions.stem: Among the deletion syndromes discussed, which is best described as a nonrecurrent structural variant in which most cases are sporadic and the breakpoints and extent of the deletion are highly variable?

explanation.summary: Cri du chat is a nonrecurrent variant with sporadic cases and variable breakpoints, though a critical 5p region is deleted in all patients.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cri du chat syndrome is an example of a nonrecurrent chromosomal variant: most cases are sporadic and the breakpoints and extent of the deleted segment of 5p are highly variable. Research has nonetheless identified a critical region missing in all patients with the phenotype.

whyWrongByChoice.B: 22q11.2 deletion syndrome is a recurrent structural variant with a consistent ~3 Mb deletion.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Williams syndrome is also a recurrent variant, with a consistent 7q11.23 deletion.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Klinefelter syndrome is a numerical alteration (47,XXY), not a variable subchromosomal deletion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Turner syndrome is a numerical alteration (45,X), not a nonrecurrent deletion.

questions.topicName: Numerical and Structural Chromosomal Alterations (2/3) - Subchromosomal Deletion Syndromes

questions.stem: A child with a confirmed chromosomal microdeletion syndrome is found to have an elevated serum calcium concentration. This laboratory finding is most consistent with which of the following syndromes?

explanation.summary: Hypercalcemia is characteristic of Williams syndrome, in contrast to the hypocalcemia of 22q11.2 deletion syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: Patients with Williams syndrome (7q11.23 deletion) can have increased calcium levels in the blood. This helps distinguish it from 22q11.2 deletion syndrome, which is instead associated with hypocalcemia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: 22q11.2 deletion syndrome is associated with hypocalcemia, not hypercalcemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cri du chat syndrome is characterized by microcephaly and a high-pitched cry, not a distinctive calcium abnormality.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Klinefelter syndrome is a numerical sex-chromosome disorder and is not defined by hypercalcemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Turner syndrome is a numerical sex-chromosome disorder and is not defined by hypercalcemia.

questions.topicName: Numerical and Structural Chromosomal Alterations (2/3) - Subchromosomal Deletion Syndromes

questions.stem: A geneticist describes a patient whose body contains two cell populations that differ in genetic sequence but can both be traced back to the same fertilized egg. Which of the following terms best describes this finding?

explanation.summary: Mosaicism is the presence in an individual of at least two genetically distinct cell lineages derived from a single zygote.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick defines mosaicism as the presence in an individual or tissue of at least two cell lineages that differ genetically but are derived from a single zygote. It arises from a post-conception genetic event during mitotic cell division, producing a clonal population of genetically distinct cells.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chimerism describes cell populations that arise from two different zygotes, not a single one, so it fails the single-zygote criterion.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Aneuploidy is an abnormal chromosome number and is a type of mutation, not a description of two distinct lineages from one zygote.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Polyploidy is having complete extra sets of chromosomes and does not describe coexisting genetically distinct lineages.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nondisjunction is a mechanism that can cause mosaicism, but it names the event, not the coexistence of two lineages.

questions.stem: A 14-year-old girl is evaluated for delayed onset of puberty. Karyotype testing reveals 46,XX in approximately half of the cells examined and 45,X in the other half. Which of the following is most likely responsible for the two karyotypes observed in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nondisjunction occurred in a single cell early in development

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nondisjunction occurred simultaneously in half the cells during early development

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mosaicism was present at fertilization and became generalized

choices.label: D
choices.text: Germline mosaicism was present in one of the parents

choices.label: E
choices.text: Meiotic nondisjunction in the oocyte produced a uniformly 45,X conceptus

explanation.summary: Mosaicism (here, mosaic Turner syndrome) arises when a post-zygotic event in a single cell establishes a clonal lineage that coexists with the original line.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mosaicism occurs when a genetic event, such as mitotic nondisjunction, happens in one cell that then continues to divide, giving rise to a clonal lineage. A single early nondisjunction event losing an X establishes a 45,X line that coexists with the 46,XX line, producing mosaic Turner syndrome.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Simultaneous nondisjunction in many separate cells is far less likely than a single event that clonally expands to produce a large fraction of cells.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mosaicism always arises after fertilization within the affected individual; it cannot be present at the moment of fertilization.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Germline mosaicism in a parent transmits a mutation that would make the child fully (uniformly) affected, not mosaic with two karyotypes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Meiotic nondisjunction would yield a uniform 45,X karyotype in all cells, not a two-line mosaic pattern.

questions.stem: An 18-year-old woman reports muscle fatigue and weakness climbing stairs, with elevated serum creatine kinase. Neither parent nor her siblings are affected, but genetic testing shows she and her mother are both carriers of a pathogenic variant in the DMD gene (X-linked recessive Duchenne muscular dystrophy). Which of the following most likely explains the appearance of symptoms in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The X chromosome carrying the mutation is active in a high proportion of her muscle cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: She has a de novo mutation in the second copy of the DMD gene

choices.label: C
choices.text: She has monosomy X (Turner syndrome) and only one X chromosome

choices.label: D
choices.text: The pathogenic variant blocks X inactivation so she expresses extra dystrophin

choices.label: E
choices.text: The mutation is imprinted and therefore always expressed

explanation.summary: A manifesting heterozygote results when skewed X inactivation leaves the mutation-bearing X active in a large fraction of the relevant tissue.
explanation.whyCorrect: By chance, X inactivation can be skewed so that the normal X is silenced in most muscle cells, leaving the mutant DMD X active in a high proportion of cells. Such a female carrier expresses the phenotype and is called a manifesting heterozygote.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A second-hit mutation would be detected on testing and would cause the full Duchenne phenotype rather than mild, carrier-level symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Turner syndrome would show a single X and additional systemic features; her karyotype and family testing point to a heterozygous carrier state.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mutations in X-linked protein-coding genes do not prevent X inactivation, and reduced (not extra) dystrophin causes disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: DMD is not an imprinted gene; the partial expression is explained by the X-inactivation pattern, not imprinting.

questions.stem: A couple with no signs of disease has two children who both carry the identical transition mutation in the FBN1 gene causing Marfan syndrome, an autosomal dominant disorder. Detailed examination and blood-based genetic testing of both parents are negative for the mutation. Which of the following is the most likely explanation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Two independent de novo mutations coincidentally occurred in the children

choices.label: B
choices.text: Germline mosaicism is present in one of the parents

choices.label: C
choices.text: The condition is actually autosomal recessive in this family

choices.label: D
choices.text: One parent carries the mutation but has non-penetrant disease

choices.label: E
choices.text: Confined placental mosaicism affected both pregnancies

explanation.summary: Unaffected parents with more than one child sharing the same dominant mutation suggest germline (gonadal) mosaicism in a parent.
explanation.whyCorrect: Germline mosaicism should be considered when unaffected parents have multiple affected offspring with the same mutation. A parent whose mutation is confined to the germline is phenotypically normal and tests negative in blood, yet transmits the FBN1 variant, so future offspring have an increased recurrence risk.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Two independent identical de novo mutations at the same site in siblings is exceedingly improbable and does not fit the shared identical variant.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Marfan syndrome is autosomal dominant; a single mutant FBN1 allele is sufficient to cause disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Both parents tested negative on genetic testing, so an unexpressed but present constitutional mutation is not supported.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Confined placental mosaicism involves only extraembryonic tissue and would not transmit a mutation to the children themselves.

questions.stem: An individual is found to have pure somatic mosaicism for a pathogenic mutation. Which of the following cell types would NOT be expected to carry the mutation?

explanation.summary: In pure somatic mosaicism, only non-germline tissues can be affected; gametes are spared.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pure somatic mosaicism indicates that the mutation is present in some somatic tissues but not in the gametes. Oocytes are part of the germline and therefore would not carry the mutation, so the phenotype is not transmitted to offspring.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Blood is a somatic tissue and can harbor the mutation in somatic mosaicism.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Kidney is a somatic tissue and may be affected.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Skin is a somatic tissue and may be affected.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Skeletal muscle is a somatic tissue and may be affected.

questions.stem: A couple's first child is diagnosed with segmental neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1), with pigmentation changes and benign tumors restricted to only some tissues, indicating mosaicism for an NF1 mutation. NF1 is autosomal dominant with nearly complete penetrance, and neither parent has any phenotype. Which of the following statements is most accurate regarding risk assessment for this family?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The parents are at increased risk of having another child with segmental NF1

choices.label: B
choices.text: All subsequent offspring of the parents will be affected with segmental NF1

choices.label: C
choices.text: The parents are at increased risk of having another child with full (nonsegmental) NF1

choices.label: D
choices.text: The child's future offspring are at increased risk of having segmental NF1

choices.label: E
choices.text: The child's future offspring are at increased risk of having full (nonsegmental) NF1

explanation.summary: Segmental disease reflects a post-zygotic mutation; if the germline is involved, offspring inherit a constitutional mutation and develop the full disorder.
explanation.whyCorrect: Segmental NF1 results from a post-zygotic mutation, so the parents are not at increased recurrence risk. Because germline involvement in the child cannot be excluded, the child may transmit the mutant allele; since NF1 is autosomal dominant, such offspring would have full (nonsegmental) NF1.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The mutation arose after fertilization in the child, so the parents' recurrence risk is not increased.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Mosaicism arose post-fertilization in the child; subsequent siblings are not obligately affected.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The parents did not transmit the mutation and are not at increased risk for either segmental or full NF1.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Segmental disease requires a post-zygotic event in that individual; an inherited constitutional mutation produces full, not segmental, disease.

questions.stem: During prenatal evaluation, chorionic villus sampling reveals a trisomic cell line, but follow-up amniocentesis and the newborn's karyotype are normal. The pregnancy was complicated by intrauterine growth restriction. Which of the following best explains these findings?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Maternal cell contamination of the sample

explanation.summary: Confined placental mosaicism is an abnormal cell line limited to extraembryonic tissue, sometimes causing delayed fetal growth.
explanation.whyCorrect: In confined placental mosaicism, the mutation or aneuploidy is found only in extraembryonic tissue and not in the embryo proper. It can result in a normal outcome or, with trisomy mosaicism, delayed fetal growth, matching the trisomic villi with a normal fetus and growth restriction.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Pure germline mosaicism is restricted to gametes and would not appear in placental villi or cause fetal growth restriction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Somatic mosaicism in the fetus would be detectable in fetal-derived cells such as amniocytes, which were normal here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Maternal contamination would typically produce a normal maternal karyotype, not a trisomic line, and does not explain growth restriction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Constitutional trisomy would be present in fetal cells and the neonate, whose karyotypes were normal.

questions.stem: The X inactivation center contains an unusual non-coding RNA gene that acts as a master regulator of X inactivation. It is expressed from the allele on the inactive X and produces a long RNA that remains in the nucleus coating that chromosome. Which of the following genes is described?

explanation.summary: XIST is the non-coding RNA master regulator that silences the inactive X.
explanation.whyCorrect: The X inactivation center contains XIST, an unusual non-coding RNA gene expressed from the inactive X. Its long ncRNA stays in the nucleus in close association with the inactive X and drives epigenetic silencing of most of its genes.

whyWrongByChoice.B: SRY is the Y-linked testis-determining gene and has no role in X inactivation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: FMR1 is the fragile X gene, an X-linked protein-coding gene, not the inactivation regulator.
whyWrongByChoice.D: FBN1 encodes fibrillin-1 and is associated with Marfan syndrome, unrelated to X inactivation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: DMD encodes dystrophin and is an X-linked disease gene, not the master regulator of inactivation.

questions.stem: A buccal smear from a phenotypic female shows a single condensed heterochromatic mass at the periphery of the nucleus in interphase cells. This structure most directly represents which of the following?

explanation.summary: The Barr body is the cytologically visible inactive X in interphase cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cytologically, the inactive X chromosome is visible as a heterochromatic mass called the Barr body in interphase cells. It reflects the epigenetically silenced, condensed inactive X.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The active X remains euchromatic and is not seen as a condensed peripheral mass.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The nucleolus is a distinct organelle associated with ribosomal RNA synthesis, not the inactivated X.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A single Barr body indicates one inactivated X in a female; it is not a Y chromosome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The mass specifically corresponds to the inactivated X, not generic autosomal heterochromatin.

questions.stem: A patient has 46 chromosomes with one structurally abnormal X (an isochromosome). Studies show that in nearly all of her cells the same (abnormal) X chromosome is inactivated, rather than a random pattern. Which of the following best explains this nonrandom inactivation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Selection against genetically unbalanced cells favors survival of those inactivating the abnormal X

choices.label: B
choices.text: The XIST gene is present only on the normal X chromosome

choices.label: C
choices.text: The abnormal X lacks a functional centromere

choices.label: D
choices.text: Random inactivation always preferentially silences the paternal X

choices.label: E
choices.text: Genomic imprinting silences all structurally abnormal chromosomes

explanation.summary: Skewed inactivation of a structurally abnormal X reflects postnatal selection against genetically unbalanced cells.
