explanation.whyCorrect: The initial inactivation event early in development is random, but in nearly all patients with unbalanced structural X abnormalities the abnormal chromosome is the inactive X. The observed skew reflects selection against genetically unbalanced cells that have reduced viability.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The pattern is driven by cell selection, not by absence of XIST from the abnormal X.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The chromosome is described as an X with a structural abnormality that persists in cells; a missing centromere is not the mechanism given.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Random X inactivation does not systematically favor the paternal X; typical distributions are roughly balanced.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Imprinting affects a small subset of specific genes and does not silence whole abnormal chromosomes.

questions.stem: A child has somatic mosaicism for an autosomal dominant disorder, with some tissues affected and others normal. Regarding this child's own future reproductive risk, which of the following is most accurate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: There is no reproductive risk because mosaicism is never heritable

choices.label: B
choices.text: All of the child's offspring will be mosaic like the child

choices.label: C
choices.text: The child is at increased risk of having fully affected offspring because germline involvement cannot be excluded

choices.label: D
choices.text: Risk exists only if the child's future partner is also a carrier

choices.label: E
choices.text: Risk is zero because the mutation arose after fertilization

explanation.summary: A mosaic individual is at increased risk of fully affected offspring because their germ cells may also carry the mutation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Somatic mosaicism confirms the mutation is present in some tissues, but it does not tell us whether the germline is involved. Because germ cells may carry the mutation, the child is at increased risk of transmitting the mutant allele, and for an autosomal dominant disorder such offspring would be fully affected.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mosaicism can involve the germline, so it is not universally non-heritable.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Offspring inheriting a constitutional mutation would be fully affected, not mosaic.
whyWrongByChoice.D: For an autosomal dominant disorder, a single transmitted mutant allele is sufficient; a carrier partner is not required.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A post-fertilization event can still affect germ cells, so the risk is not zero.

questions.stem: A child has features of Down syndrome that are subtler than typically expected. Karyotype analysis shows two cell lines: some cells with trisomy 21 and some with normal disomy 21. Compared with a child with full (nonmosaic) trisomy 21, this child is most likely to have which of the following?

choices.label: C
choices.text: An identical phenotype in all cases

choices.label: E
choices.text: A phenotype only if the germline is involved

explanation.summary: Mosaic Down syndrome, a mixture of disomic and trisomic cells, typically produces a less severe phenotype.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mosaic Down syndrome is a mixture of cells that are disomic and trisomic for chromosome 21, seen in about 1-3% of trisomy 21 live births. These individuals typically have a less severe phenotype because a proportion of cells have the normal chromosome complement.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The presence of normal disomic cells generally lessens, rather than worsens, the phenotype.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The phenotype is variable and usually milder, not identical to full trisomy 21.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Trisomic cells are present, so an affected phenotype is still expected, just typically milder.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The somatic trisomic cell line drives the phenotype; germline involvement is not required for features to appear.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old boy is evaluated for poor feeding, failure to thrive, and developmental delay. Examination shows low-set ears, a small chin, and a heart murmur; echocardiography reveals a ventricular septal defect. Recurrent infections and low serum calcium are noted. The physician suspects a 22q11.2 microdeletion syndrome. Which technique is the most appropriate to confirm this specific microdeletion?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Polymerase chain reaction of the FXN gene

explanation.summary: FISH is the targeted method of choice for confirming a clinically suspected microdeletion such as 22q11.2.
explanation.whyCorrect: FISH uses fluorescently labeled probes complementary to a specific locus, so when a particular microdeletion is already suspected it can confirm the deletion (e.g., one red 22q11 signal alongside a normal control probe). It resolves submicroscopic changes invisible on a standard karyotype.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A standard G-banded karyotype only detects abnormalities of roughly 5-10 Mb or larger and cannot resolve a submicroscopic 22q11 microdeletion.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Southern blot detects specific sequences and repeat expansions but is a low-throughput, radiolabel-dependent method not used to confirm this microdeletion.
whyWrongByChoice.D: PCR of FXN targets the Friedreich ataxia gene and is unrelated to a 22q11 microdeletion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An electrolyte panel documents hypocalcemia but does not establish the underlying chromosomal diagnosis.

questions.stem: A newborn has a flattened facial profile, a small nose with a flat bridge, a protruding tongue, and a single transverse palmar crease. Echocardiography demonstrates an atrioventricular septal defect. A cytogenetic study visualizes all chromosomes arranged in pairs and shows three copies of chromosome 21. Which technique produced this result?

explanation.summary: Karyotyping visualizes all chromosomes as pairs and readily reveals aneuploidy such as trisomy 21.
explanation.whyCorrect: A G-banded karyotype arranges stained metaphase chromosomes into numbered pairs, allowing assessment of chromosome number and structure. Down syndrome appears as three copies of chromosome 21 (47,XX,+21 or 47,XY,+21), which was the finding that first linked Down syndrome to trisomy 21.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Array CGH detects copy number variation as a dosage ratio but does not display chromosomes as visible pairs.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Southern blot detects specific DNA sequences on a membrane, not whole-chromosome number.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Western blot detects proteins, not chromosomes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: FISH highlights only a targeted locus rather than displaying the full set of paired chromosomes.

questions.stem: In preparing a G-banded karyotype, cultured cells are treated with a chemical that halts cell division at the stage where chromosomes are most condensed and aligned at the cell's equator, maximizing their visibility. Which agent is used for this purpose?

explanation.summary: Colchicine arrests dividing cells at metaphase, when chromosomes are maximally condensed and best visualized.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cultured cells are treated with colchicine to stop cell division at metaphase, the stage at which chromosomes are most condensed and aligned at the cell equator. This makes them highly defined and ideal for analysis before lysis and staining.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Trypsin is a protease that partially digests histones and chromosomal proteins to help reveal banding; it does not arrest the cell cycle.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Giemsa is the dye mixture that produces the banding pattern after the cells are already arrested.
whyWrongByChoice.D: DAPI is a blue DNA counterstain used in FISH, not a metaphase-arresting agent in karyotyping.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Restriction endonucleases cut DNA for methods like Southern blot and are not used to arrest cells.

questions.stem: During interpretation of a G-banded chromosome, a technologist notes darkly stained bands (G-positive) with Giemsa. Compared with the lighter G-negative bands, which set of properties best characterizes these dark bands?

choices.label: A
choices.text: GC-rich, gene-rich, euchromatin with active transcription

explanation.summary: Dark G-positive bands are AT-rich, gene-poor heterochromatin.
explanation.whyCorrect: Giemsa binds more strongly to AT-rich DNA, producing dark bands that are gene-poor and generally associated with heterochromatin. Light G-negative bands are the opposite: GC-rich, gene-rich euchromatin with more active transcription.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This describes the light G-negative bands, not the dark bands.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Dark bands are AT-rich, not GC-rich.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dark bands are gene-poor heterochromatin, not gene-rich euchromatin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Dark bands reflect broad AT-rich, gene-poor regions, not exclusively telomeric repeats.

questions.stem: A karyotype from a male patient shows material exchanged between chromosome 9 and chromosome 22, with both chromosomes visibly altered. The two homologs of chromosomes 9 and 22 are each affected on one member. Which description best fits this abnormality?

choices.label: C
choices.text: t(9;22), a translocation involving two chromosomes

choices.label: E
choices.text: A submicroscopic microdeletion detectable only by FISH

explanation.summary: Involvement of two different chromosomes exchanging material indicates a translocation, t(9;22).
explanation.whyCorrect: Because two chromosomes (9 and 22) are both altered, the finding represents a translocation between them, t(9;22). A deletion or duplication would impact only a single chromosome, and the presence of a Y confirms the patient is male rather than having Turner syndrome.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A deletion of 22q11.2 affects only one chromosome, but here two chromosomes are altered.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 45,X (Turner) would show a single X and no Y; this patient is a male with a Y chromosome.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A duplication affects one chromosome and would not explain changes to both 9 and 22.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The exchange between two whole chromosomes is visible on the karyotype and is not a submicroscopic, FISH-only lesion.

questions.stem: A geneticist wants an unbiased, genome-wide survey of chromosomal copy number variation in a child with developmental delay, intellectual disability, and dysmorphic features and a normal karyotype. Patient DNA is labeled with a red dye and reference DNA with a green dye, then co-hybridized to a microarray. In a well where the green signal exceeds the red, what has occurred in the patient's DNA at that locus?

explanation.summary: In array CGH, a green-dominant (weaker red) signal means the patient DNA failed to bind, indicating a deletion.
explanation.whyCorrect: Array CGH compares red-labeled test DNA to green-labeled reference DNA and reads the red-to-green ratio. A stronger green than red signal means the patient (test) DNA did not bind the probe, implying the sequence is deleted at that locus. A red-dominant well indicates duplication, and a yellow well indicates no CNV.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A duplication would produce a red-dominant, not green-dominant, signal.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Absence of copy number change gives a balanced yellow signal (equal red and green).
whyWrongByChoice.D: Array CGH cannot detect balanced translocations, which do not change copy number.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Array CGH measures copy number dosage and does not identify single-base point mutations.

questions.stem: On an array CGH plot, a genomic region returns a DNA dosage score of -1. Which interpretation is correct?

explanation.summary: A dosage score of -1 on array CGH corresponds to a heterozygous deletion with one remaining copy.
explanation.whyCorrect: The array CGH dosage scale runs from -2 to +2: -2 is a homozygous deletion (0 copies), -1 is a heterozygous deletion (1 copy), 0 is normal (2 copies), +1 is a duplication with 3 copies, and +2 is a duplication with 4 copies.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Zero copies corresponds to a score of -2, not -1.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Normal two-copy dosage corresponds to a score of 0.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Three copies corresponds to a score of +1.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Four copies corresponds to a score of +2.

questions.stem: A 2-month-old girl has poor feeding, recurrent vomiting, and failure to thrive. She has microcephaly, epicanthal folds, and a ventricular septal defect. Initial testing shows a normal 46,XX karyotype, but a chromosomal microarray subsequently detects a 1.5 Mb deletion at 7q11.23. Which property of array CGH explains why it detected an abnormality that the karyotype missed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It can detect balanced translocations that karyotyping cannot

choices.label: B
choices.text: It has much higher resolution, detecting CNVs

choices.label: C
choices.text: It amplifies the deleted region to make it visible

choices.label: D
choices.text: It requires prior knowledge of the exact deletion location

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is more sensitive for low-level mosaicism

explanation.summary: Array CGH has far higher resolution than karyotyping, detecting CNVs down to ~50-200 kb.
explanation.whyCorrect: A standard karyotype only reliably detects abnormalities of about 5-10 Mb or larger, so a 1.5 Mb 7q11.23 deletion (Williams syndrome) can be missed. Array CGH detects copy number variants as small as approximately 50-200 kb, giving it the resolution to identify this microdeletion.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Array CGH actually cannot detect balanced translocations because they do not change copy number.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Array CGH measures hybridization dosage; it does not amplify the deleted DNA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Array CGH is an unbiased, untargeted method and does not require prior knowledge of the lesion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Array CGH has reduced, not increased, sensitivity for low-level mosaicism.

questions.stem: A researcher must accurately size a very large trinucleotide GAA repeat expansion in intron 1 of the FXN gene in a patient with progressive gait ataxia, dysarthria, and cardiomyopathy. The expansion is too long for reliable amplification by conventional PCR. Which technique is best suited to size this expansion?

explanation.summary: Southern blot can accurately size large repeat expansions that are too long for conventional PCR.
explanation.whyCorrect: Southern blotting digests genomic DNA with a restriction enzyme, separates fragments by gel electrophoresis, and detects the target with a labeled probe. It can accurately size large repeat expansions, such as the FXN GAA expansion of Friedreich ataxia, that exceed the length reliably amplified by conventional PCR.

whyWrongByChoice.A: FISH localizes a probe to a chromosomal region but does not size a triplet repeat expansion.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A karyotype lacks the resolution to measure a repeat expansion.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Array CGH measures copy number dosage, not the length of a triplet repeat.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Western blot detects proteins, not the DNA repeat length.

questions.stem: A student is comparing molecular techniques by their principal target molecule. Which pairing is correct?

explanation.summary: Western blot detects proteins; Southern detects DNA, Northern detects RNA.
explanation.whyCorrect: The blot family is distinguished by target: Southern blot detects DNA, Northern blot detects RNA, Western blot detects proteins, and Eastern blot detects post-translational modifications. Western blot remains a very common laboratory technique for protein detection.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Southern blot detects DNA, not RNA (RNA is the Northern blot target).
whyWrongByChoice.B: Northern blot detects RNA, not proteins.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Western blot detects proteins, not DNA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Post-translational modifications are the Eastern blot target, not the Southern blot target.

questions.stem: A couple with three consecutive first-trimester pregnancy losses is referred for evaluation. Both partners are phenotypically normal. Karyotype testing of both partners is recommended because balanced reciprocal and Robertsonian translocations are the major abnormalities identified in this setting. What is the primary reason a balanced translocation carrier is typically phenotypically normal yet at risk for pregnancy loss?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The rearrangement deletes a large gene-rich region

choices.label: B
choices.text: No genetic material is gained or lost, but gametes may be unbalanced

choices.label: C
choices.text: The rearrangement adds a whole extra chromosome

choices.label: D
choices.text: It produces a submicroscopic duplication detectable only by array CGH

choices.label: E
choices.text: It causes a triplet repeat expansion in the carrier

explanation.summary: Balanced translocation carriers have no net loss or gain of material but can form unbalanced gametes, causing pregnancy loss.
explanation.whyCorrect: In a balanced translocation the total genetic material is preserved, so the carrier is usually phenotypically normal. However, during meiosis the carrier can produce unbalanced gametes with missing or extra segments, leading to recurrent pregnancy loss; this is why karyotyping both partners is part of the recurrent-loss evaluation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A balanced rearrangement by definition does not delete material, which is why the carrier is unaffected.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Adding a whole chromosome is aneuploidy, not a balanced translocation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A balanced translocation does not change copy number and is actually a lesion array CGH cannot detect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Triplet repeat expansions are a distinct mechanism unrelated to balanced translocations.

questions.stem: A clinician is choosing a diagnostic test but wants a method that does NOT require prior knowledge of the suspected abnormality. Which of the following best describes the key limitation that makes FISH unsuitable when the abnormality is unknown?

choices.label: A
choices.text: FISH has very low resolution and misses small changes

choices.label: B
choices.text: FISH is a targeted method that only reveals suspected abnormalities

choices.label: D
choices.text: FISH requires digestion with a restriction enzyme

choices.label: E
choices.text: FISH can only be performed on proteins

explanation.summary: FISH is a targeted method requiring prior knowledge, so it only detects what its probe is designed to find.
explanation.whyCorrect: FISH is an 'a priori' targeted approach: a probe must be designed for a known sequence, so it only reveals suspected abnormalities. When the abnormality is unknown, an unbiased genome-wide method such as array CGH or karyotyping is needed instead.

whyWrongByChoice.A: FISH is actually a high-resolution technique that detects small changes missed by karyotyping.
whyWrongByChoice.C: FISH depends on fluorescently labeled probes; using them is a defining feature, not a limitation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Restriction digestion is a step in Southern blot, not FISH.
whyWrongByChoice.E: FISH targets DNA sequences on chromosomes, not proteins.
